1.description: (Color online) Parallel Trends Analysis Notes. This figure plots coefficients for the relation between different event days relative to spring DST and the relative forecast accuracy of novice forecasters compared with professional forecasters. Each regression includes the same control variables included in columns (2) and (4) of Table 3 and interacts the Nonprofessional indicator variable with a series of 11 different 2-calendar-day event windows surrounding spring DST (excluding weekends). Nonprofessional is an indicator variable equal to one when the analyst’s professional background reported in Estimize is listed as “Non Professional.” Each point in the figure represents the coefficient on the interaction of Nonprofessional and an indicator for one of the two-day event windows. For example, the point at 0 on the x -axis reflects the coefficient on the interaction of the Nonprofessional variable and an indicator variable for the two calendar days beginning on the Monday following spring DST Sunday. The dependent variable, Price-Scaled Accuracy , is (the negative of) analyst i ’s absolute forecast error, computed as − 1 × | 𝐹 𝑜 𝑟 𝑒 𝑐 𝑎 𝑠 𝑡 𝑒 𝑑 𝐸 𝑃 𝑆 − 𝐴 𝑐 𝑡 𝑢 𝑎 𝑙 𝐸 𝑃 𝑆 | × 1 0 0 − 1 × | Forecasted EPS − Actual EPS | × 100 , scaled by the firm’s share price 100 calendar days prior to the forecast. The sample of forecasts is restricted to forecasts between 2013 and 2019 issued during the two-day DST treatment window or the 30 calendar days on each side of the treatment window. The sample is further restricted to forecasts (i) not flagged by Estimize as unreliable, (ii) issued for earnings periods that receive at least 20 forecasts, (iii) issued no later than the earnings announcement date, and (iv) with horizons less than 200 days. The event window indicator variables for the event days between −29 and −13 relative to spring DST Sunday and the event days between +16 and +33 are excluded from the regression; these event days represent the comparison group for the interaction terms. Standard errors are two-way clustered at the analyst and firm levels. The vertical lines represent 90% confidence intervals.
